rule_text: A trader should not assume a method has predictive power based solely on past backtest performance; profitable historical results are necessary but not sufficient evidence, as they may result from luck or upward bias rather than genuine edge.
entry_conditions: Method shows positive backtest results
Out-of-sample performance confirmed over minimum 2 separate hold-out periods
Profitability consistent across at least 3 different market regimes
Sharpe ratio >= 1.5 in both in-sample and out-of-sample data
No optimization curve-fitting detected (parameter sensitivity analysis passed)
exit_conditions: Live trading performance deviates >20% from backtest expectations
Out-of-sample metrics fall below baseline

source_citation: Evidence-Based Technical Analysis, David Aronson, Ch.0, p.10-528
raw_chunk_text:
The following are a few of the findings from academic TA. It shows
that, when approached in a rigorous and intellectually honest manner, TA
is a worthwhile area of study.
• Expert chartists are unable
rule_text: A trader should employ simple objective trend-following indicators in trending markets (commodities and forex) because empirical evidence demonstrates exploitable trends and the economic rationale that trend-following speculators provide valuable hedging services.
entry_conditions: Simple moving average crossover: price > SMA(50) AND price > SMA(200)
Trend confirmation: at least 3 consecutive higher highs over past 20 bars
Volume confirmation: current volume > 20-bar average volume * 1.0
exit_conditions: Exit when price closes below the 50-period SMA
Exit after 20 bars in trade regardless of profit/loss

source_citation: Evidence-Based Technical Analysis, David Aronson, Ch.2, p.10-528
raw_chunk_text:
• Simple technical rules used individually and in combinations can
yield statistically and economically significant profits when applied to
stock market averages composed of relatively young companies
rule_text: A trader should use momentum and relative strength indicators to identify and trade persistent trends in stocks and sectors, because these signals have statistically significant predictive power over medium-term horizons.
entry_conditions: Simple momentum indicator (e.g., price > SMA) confirms uptrend
Relative strength rank in top 50th percentile of universe
Entry signal confirmed for 2+ consecutive periods
exit_conditions: Exit after 12 months (maximum hold period)
Exit if momentum indicator turns negative
Exit if relative strength falls below 30th percentile

rule_text: Price proximity to recent highs (52-week high differential) is a predictive indicator of future relative outperformance; stocks trading near their 52-week highs tend to outperform those trading further below them.
entry_conditions: Current Price >= (52-Week High × 0.90)
Price has not closed below this threshold for 5+ consecutive days
exit_conditions: Price closes below 52-week high by more than 10%
Position duration exceeds 6 months

rule_text: A trader should subject all trading ideas to rigorous objective testing and eliminate those that fail empirical validation, rather than emotionally committing to untested hypotheses.
entry_conditions: Backtested strategy passes significance test at p < 0.05
Minimum 500 trades in sample to establish statistical power
Win rate exceeds 52% over full sample period
Out-of-sample performance degrades no more than 10% from in-sample results
exit_conditions: Live trading shows p-value > 0.10 over 100+ trades
Win rate drops below 48% in live results
Drawdown exceeds historical backtest maximum by 25%

diffusion indicator called Big Mo. With two thresholds and two possible
directional modes (up/down), six mutually exclusive conditions are de-
fined. A binary rule could be derived from such an analys
rule_text: A trader should enter long positions when a diffusion indicator exceeds a threshold AND shows positive directional momentum, as this regime is associated with significantly higher returns than when the indicator is elevated but momentum is negative.
entry_conditions: Big Mo diffusion indicator > 65
Big Mo recent change is positive AND sustained for N >= 2 periods
Exclude entry if indicator was > 60 but changing negative in prior bar
exit_conditions: Big Mo falls below 60

Time-based: exit after 252 trading days (annualization window)

Given that the veracity of traditional TA maybe questionable, it is de-
sirable to test rules that are contrary to the traditional interpretation. In

rule_text: A trader should test the inverse of traditional technical analysis rules to determine whether reversed signals have predictive value, since traditional patterns may actually predict opposite outcomes or have no predictive value at all.
entry_conditions: Traditional TA pattern identified with high confidence (e.g., strong moving average crossover)
Take opposite position (e.g., sell if traditional rule says buy)
Require inverse signal to persist for minimum lookback period
exit_conditions: Fixed stop loss at 2x traditional pattern risk level
Take profit at 1x traditional pattern risk level
Exit after 20 bars regardless of P&L

rule_text: Adjust observed trading rule returns by subtracting the expected return of a non-predictive benchmark rule with identical position bias to isolate true predictive power and avoid false superiority claims caused by market trend interaction with position bias.
entry_conditions: Rule generates observed positive returns
Rule position bias (% long allocation) is calculated
Non-predictive benchmark rule with same position bias is constructed
Benchmark return is subtracted from observed rule return
Excess return exceeds 150 basis points (1.5%)
Excess return persists across 80% of test periods
exit_conditions: Excess return drops below threshold
Position bias drifts >10% from historical average

Trading Costs
Should trading costs be taken into account in rule back-tests? If the intent
is to use the rule on a stand-alone basis for trading, the answer is clearly
yes. For example, rules that sig
rule_text: When backtesting a rule intended for stand-alone trading, adjust performance metrics by deducting trading costs (commissions and slippage) proportional to signal frequency, because high-frequency reversal rules incur disproportionately higher costs that must be accounted for in comparative performance evaluation.
entry_conditions: Rule generates a signal
Backtest mode = STAND_ALONE_DEPLOYMENT
exit_conditions: Deduct accumulated costs from net profit before reporting final performance

rule_text: A trader should reject or heavily scrutinize trading signals derived from subjective technical analysis methods that produce vague pattern definitions and interpretable predictions, because weak evidence that is open to multiple interpretations creates cognitive bias and illusory validity rather than genuine predictive power.
entry_conditions: Signal originates from mechanically-defined, objective pattern definition (not human interpretation)
Pattern has published, peer-reviewed or empirically-backtested statistical edge
Entry and exit rules are specified before trade initiation with zero discretion
exit_conditions: Pre-specified mechanical exit triggered
Pattern fails objective definition criteria mid-trade

The Law of Large Numbers tells us that sample size is of paramount im-
portance because it determines the confidence we can have in inferences
based on a sample of data. Also known as the Law of Avera
rule_text: A trader should only act on technical analysis conclusions when the sample size of observations is large enough to reliably reflect the true characteristics of the process, not merely patterns that match intuitive expectations of non-randomness.

exit_conditions: Exit if pattern performance degrades below 55% win rate in new market regime

One challenge that faces the real-world chartist is the fact that actual
price oscillations do not trace out clearly identifiable peaks and troughs.
Rather, peaks and troughs occur at numerous oscilla
rule_text: Use a percentage filter (zigzag indicator) to objectively isolate price peaks and troughs at a consistent scale, reducing subjective interpretation bias in real-time pattern recognition.
entry_conditions: Price movement exceeds filter threshold from last identified peak or trough
Peak or trough confirmed by zigzag indicator at selected percentage level
Pattern emerges only after scale-filtering is applied consistently
exit_conditions: Price reversal exceeds 5% from entry in opposite direction
Pattern invalidated by break of filtered trough (long) or peak (short)

rule_text: A valid head-and-shoulders reversal pattern must occur within an uptrend where both shoulders remain above their prior peaks and troughs, establishing a qualified setup before pattern completion.

Neckline deviation ≤ 5% of pattern height
Pattern formation time ≥ 20 periods
exit_conditions: Exit on neckline break with 1.5x pattern height profit target
Stop loss at right shoulder peak + 1 ATR

rule_text: A trader should validate head-and-shoulders patterns only when they exhibit vertical symmetry, defined by shoulder price levels and trough price levels maintaining acceptable proximity to their respective midpoint references, rejecting patterns with necklines that are too steeply sloped.
entry_conditions: Right shoulder price (E) > Left midpoint (X) by at least 2%
Left trough price (B) < Left midpoint (X) by at least 2%
Left shoulder price (A) > Right midpoint (Y) by at least 2%
Right trough price (D) < Right midpoint (Y) by at least 2%
exit_conditions: Price breaks above neckline
Pattern fails any symmetry condition by more than tolerance

rule_text: A trader should validate technical analysis methods through statistical analysis across multiple datasets before deploying them, because TA methods display highly variable performance and extrapolation from historical patterns is inherently uncertain.
entry_conditions: TA method passes statistical significance test at p < 0.01 across 3+ independent datasets
Method shows consistent profitability across trending AND ranging AND high-volatility regimes
Out-of-sample performance matches in-sample performance within 10% drawdown variance
Minimum 200+ independent trades generated in backtests
exit_conditions: Live performance degrades below backtest results by more than 15%
Method fails statistical significance in new market regime

rule_text: When selecting a trading rule from multiple tested candidates, use the rule with the highest observed performance only if it is supported by a sufficient sample size of observations to reduce data-mining bias and improve out-of-sample generalization.
entry_conditions: Rule undergoes backtest across minimum 500+ trades or 10+ years of data
Rule outperformance is statistically significant at p < 0.01 level

Market regime fundamentally changes (detected via regime filter)

rule_text: Reduce data-mining bias by limiting the number of rules tested during backtesting; testing fewer rules decreases the probability of spurious findings due to chance.

rule_text: A trader should require a minimum sample size of observations before accepting the performance of a rule, because insufficient data leads to data-mining bias and inflated expected returns that do not generalize.
entry_conditions: Rule candidate identified via parameter search
Historical backtest data span >= 24 months
Mean return computed from >= 24 monthly observations
exit_conditions: Do not deploy rule if sample size < 24 months
Re-validate every 12 months with fresh data

rule_text: A trading rule's observed performance is only a reliable indicator of future expected return when it has been tested on a sufficiently large number of observations; insufficient data makes observed returns virtually useless for predicting real performance.
entry_conditions: Rule has been backtested on at least 2,000+ historical observations
Observed return exceeds universe average by at least 3x
Statistical confidence interval width is less than 5% of mean return
exit_conditions: Out-of-sample performance falls below 50% of in-sample observed return
Live trading performance deviates significantly from backtest

rule_text: A trader should require a minimum sample size of observations before trusting observed strategy performance, because small samples allow randomness to dominate merit and inflate backtested returns above expected reality.
entry_conditions: Strategy has been tested on at least 800+ monthly observations (67+ years of data)
Observed performance does not exceed expected performance by more than 15%
ATR parameter count tested is ≤5 (limiting optimization degrees of freedom)
exit_conditions: Out-of-sample testing shows observed performance drops below 80% of backtest level

rule_text: A trader should require a larger sample size of historical observations when evaluating and selecting trading rules, because larger samples produce narrower sampling distributions and reduce data-mining bias, thereby improving the probability that backtested performance will persist into live trading.

Sampling distribution shows fat tails (kurtosis > 3)

rule_text: Price overreactions and underreactions to news events create predictable systematic price movements back toward rational levels, which can be exploited as they drift toward their justified fundamental value.
entry_conditions: Major news event (earnings, economic data, corporate action) has occurred
Price has moved 1.5-2.0 standard deviations from pre-news level in one direction
Price has reversed and closed back through the 50% retracement of the overreaction move
Volume confirms the reversal
exit_conditions: Price reaches 75% of the distance back to the pre-news rational level
Stop loss: 0.5 ATR below entry

rule_text: A trader should actively avoid herding behavior and benchmark-hugging that emerges from fear of underperformance, and instead maintain portfolio/position independence from crowd consensus.
entry_conditions: Position is contemplated based on recent outperformance or benchmark inclusion
Position would increase overlap with benchmark to >60% of portfolio weights
exit_conditions: Exit if position was added primarily due to recent performance (6-month lookback)
Reduce if benchmark overlap exceeds 55%

rule_text: A trader should size each position using the Kelly Criterion formula—wagering only the optimal fraction of capital based on win probability and win/loss ratio—because exceeding this fraction increases risk without improving capital growth rate.
entry_conditions: Win probability and win/loss ratio have been empirically estimated from recent trades or backtested edge
Confidence in the edge estimate is moderate or lower
exit_conditions: Exit trade according to predetermined stop-loss and profit target rules (position sizing does not determine exits)

rule_text: A trader should form a long position in assets ranked in the highest performance percentile and a short position in assets ranked in the lowest performance percentile over a lookback period, then measure predictive power by comparing forward returns between the two extreme portfolios.
entry_conditions: Calculate prior 6-month returns for asset universe
Rank assets by 6-month return
Long position: assets ranked 70th–100th percentile
Short position: assets ranked 0–30th percentile
Hold for 1 month (one forward period)
exit_conditions: Exit at end of 1-month forward period
Rebalance and reassess momentum rankings

• Small capitalization effect: A stock’s total market capitalization,
defined as the number of shares outstanding multiplied by its price
per share, is predictive of future returns.45 Stocks in a port
rule_text: Stocks with lower valuation multiples (market cap, P/E ratio, price-to-book value) outperform those with higher multiples, enabling systematic selection of securities with superior expected returns.
entry_conditions: Stock is in lowest decile (10th percentile) by market capitalization
Stock P/E ratio is in lowest quartile (25th percentile) of sector
Stock price-to-book ratio is in lowest quartile (25th percentile) of sector
All three conditions are simultaneously satisfied
exit_conditions: Position valuation metrics move above 40th percentile
Time-based exit after 12 months

rule_text: A trader should enter long positions when earnings surprises are confirmed by strong price and volume action on the announcement day, as this combination predicts significant outperformance over the following month.

Volume > 150% of 30-day average volume
Close above previous day's high (confirms directional strength)
exit_conditions: Exit at 30 calendar days post-entry
Stop loss: 8% below entry price

rule_text: A trader should buy assets with the most negative long-term trends (mean reversion reversal candidates) and simultaneously short assets with the most positive long-term trends, because extreme trends over multi-year periods tend to reverse without additional risk compensation.

exit_conditions: Exit after 3-year holding period
Or exit if asset reverses into top 50th percentile of 5-year range

rule_text: A trader should buy stocks trading near their 52-week highs because investor anchoring prevents rapid news response, creating systematic price momentum that profits from the resulting mispricing.
entry_conditions: Price >= 95% of 52-week high
Positive price momentum confirmed over last 20 trading days
Volume above 50-day average
exit_conditions: Stop loss at 52-week high breakdown (price < 90% of 52-week high)
Take profit after 10% gain from entry

ea
rule_text: Combine price momentum with trading volume confirmation to amplify signal discriminative power; high-volume momentum signals generate stronger forward returns than momentum signals alone, while high-volume negative momentum generates worse forward returns, indicating volume acts as a confirmation/amplification filter.
entry_conditions: Price momentum > +2.0 standard deviations (20-period lookback)
Trading volume > 75th percentile of 60-period rolling average
Volume > 1.5x median volume
exit_conditions: Exit when momentum crosses below +1.0 sigma
Exit when volume falls below 50th percentile for 3 consecutive bars

Trading volume > 55th percentile of 30-period rolling average
Volume > 1.0x median volume (any increase above baseline)
exit_conditions: Exit when momentum crosses below 0.0 sigma (reversal)
Exit when volume falls below 50th percentile for 1 bar

Do not exit on streak alone; wait for statistical significance

Position held for 60 bars maximum

rule_text: Exploit the anchoring bias where investors fixate on the 52-week high; when a stock trades near this anchor level, it becomes temporarily underpriced due to suppressed price response to bullish information, creating a momentum opportunity as the pricing error corrects with systematic upward movement.
entry_conditions: Price closes above 52-week high
Volume is > 120% of 20-day average
Close is > open (bullish candle)
exit_conditions: Price falls below 52-week high + 0.25% buffer
Volume dries up (< 80% of 20-day avg on 3 consecutive bars)
Stop loss at 52-week high - 2% or max adverse excursion > 3%

Investors who neglect sample size are easily deceived when trying to
decide if a time series is random or not. For example, by generalizing too
hastily from too little evidence, investors may incorrec
rule_text: A trader must require sufficient sample size of evidence before concluding that a price/earnings trend is valid, because small samples are prone to random clustering and lead to overreaction.
entry_conditions: Positive returns/growth observed for minimum 20 periods (bars/candles)
Sample size of at least 20 observations must pass statistical randomness test (p < 0.05)
Cluster must represent >40% of total lookback period to qualify as trend
exit_conditions: Close below 50-period moving average
Any return to prior mean negates trend assumption

The Diffusion of Inf
rule_text: A trader should exploit price movements that occur during periods of slow information diffusion among investors, as rapid information spread prevents technical analysis from capturing systematic profits.
entry_conditions: Information diffusion lag > 5 bars (measured by autocorrelation of returns)
Volatility clustering present (Ljung-Box test p-value < 0.10)
Price momentum persists for ≥3 consecutive bars in same direction
Volume does not spike (stays within 2-bar rolling average ± 15%)
exit_conditions: Autocorrelation drops below 0.3 (information diffusion accelerates)
Volume spike exceeds 2-bar average by >25%
2 consecutive bars reverse prior direction
Fixed time stop: 10 bars maximum hold

Volume on down days >= 125% of 15-day average volume
Countertrend signal confirmed by RSI(14) < 35 OR MACD histogram reversal
No additional reversal confirmation required
exit_conditions: Exit after 10 trading days OR
Exit on 2.5% profit OR
Exit on 2% stop-loss

rule_text: Commercial hedger positioning (net buying vs. net selling) moves counter to price direction; a trader should enter positions aligned with the hedger bias that precedes or accompanies directional moves, as hedger flows reflect underlying supply-demand imbalances.
entry_conditions: Commercial hedgers net long position > 75th percentile of trailing 252-day distribution AND price in downtrend (below 200-day MA)
Commercial hedgers net short position > 75th percentile AND price in uptrend (above 200-day MA)
Hedger positioning change must be sustained for ≥2 consecutive CFTC reporting weeks
exit_conditions: Exit when hedger positioning reverses below 50th percentile for 1 week
Exit when price breaks trend (closes beyond 200-day MA on opposite side)

rule_text: A trader should follow the price trend (buy in uptrends, sell short in downtrends) because market structure requires trend-following speculators to fulfill commercial hedger imbalances, and the market compensates this behavior through trend persistence.
entry_conditions: Price has closed above the 50-period moving average for 5+ consecutive bars (uptrend)
Price has closed below the 50-period moving average for 5+ consecutive bars (downtrend)
ADX(14) > 25 confirming trend strength
exit_conditions: Close crosses below the 50-period MA (long)
Close crosses above the 50-period MA (short)
ADX falls below 20

rule_text: A trader should only employ trend-following strategies if adequately capitalized to tolerate drawdowns of up to 30%, accepting that 50-65% of signals will be unprofitable during trendless periods, in exchange for capturing profits from large-scale trends when they occur.
entry_conditions: Account equity is at least 50x the average trade loss
Trader has explicitly accepted maximum 15% portfolio drawdown tolerance
Trend-following system win-rate is documented at ≥40%
exit_conditions: Exit all trend positions if cumulative drawdown exceeds 15%
Pause new entries if drawdown reaches 12%

rule_text: A trader should take long positions when price is above its moving average and short positions when price is below it, using a fixed lookback period to identify trend direction in futures markets.

Close price < 18-month simple moving average → SHORT
exit_conditions: Price crosses below 18-month MA (if long)

rule_text: Buy stocks that have declined sharply on declining volume over a recent period, as the low volume indicates insufficient buyers for distressed sellers, creating a systematic tendency for mean reversion in the following period.
entry_conditions: Price declined >= 8% over prior 2 weeks
Volume in decline period <= 40th percentile of 20-day average volume
Close on entry day > lowest price of decline period (confirmation of reversal initiation)
exit_conditions: Exit at 5% gain from entry
Exit after 5 trading days if no profit
Exit on stop-loss at 3% below entry

raw_chunk_text:
Limiting the case study to individual rules was detrimental for two
reasons. First, few practitioners rely on a single rule to make decisions.
Second, complex rules exploit informational synergies bet
rule_text: A trader should combine multiple rules synthesized through automated methods rather than relying on single rules, because complex rules exploit informational synergies and demonstrate higher performance even when individual component rules are unprofitable.
entry_conditions: At least 4 out of 6 component rules generate aligned signals
Each component rule has demonstrated positive expectancy in historical backtest
Composite signal strength >= 0.75 (normalized agreement score)
exit_conditions: Exit when consensus drops below 3 out of 6 rules
Exit on fixed time stop (20 bars)

Stop loss at low of entry day

Extreme Values and Transitions
The second c
rule_text: A trader should enter a position when a stationary time series reaches an extreme high or low value, or when it transitions between extremes, because these points convey actionable market information.
entry_conditions: Input series has been stationarized (CN operator applied)
Series value exceeds mean ± 2.0 standard deviations (extreme high or low)
Confirmed by transition: series crosses from one extreme region to opposite extreme region
Lookback span for stationarity calculation: 40 periods
exit_conditions: Series returns to mean ± 0.5 standard deviations
Time-based exit after 20 bars

raw_chunk_text:
Divergence Rules
Divergence analysis is a foundational concept of TA that concerns the re-
lationship between a pair of time series. It is premised on the notion that,
under normal circumstances, cert
rule_text: When two normally correlated market time series fail to move together—one reversing trend while the other continues—this divergence signals weakening of the prior shared trend and potential reversal.
entry_conditions: Price and indicator have been trending together for minimum 20 bars
One series reverses (closes beyond recent swing) while other continues original trend
Divergence persists for minimum 5 consecutive bars
Divergence magnitude exceeds 2% of recent range
exit_conditions: Series re-couple and resume shared trend direction
Position reaches 2x initial risk in profit
20 bars elapse without trend reversal

raw_chunk_text:
reverse. Another application of divergence analysis considers the price
of an instrument as one time series and its rate of change or momentum
as the second series. Price/momentum divergence analysis
rule_text: A trader should initiate a reversal trade only when both price and momentum have convincingly reversed direction and moved back into coherence (phase alignment), not when divergence first appears.
entry_conditions: Price series has reversed from prior trend (5+ bars minimum in new direction)
Momentum indicator has reversed from prior trend (5+ bars minimum in new direction)
Both series moving in same direction for minimum 5 consecutive bars
Momentum magnitude >= 60% of prior divergence magnitude
exit_conditions: Either series breaks back into previous direction
Coherence loss (series diverge again)
Stop loss at swing low/high from divergence entry point

rule_text: A trader should initiate a reversal trade only when both price and momentum have convincingly reversed direction and moved back into coherence (phase alignment), not when divergence first appears.
entry_conditions: Price series has reversed from prior trend (3+ bars minimum in new direction)
Momentum indicator has reversed from prior trend (3+ bars minimum in new direction)
Both series moving in same direction for minimum 3 consecutive bars
Momentum magnitude >= 40% of prior divergence magnitude
exit_conditions: Either series breaks back into previous direction
Coherence loss (series diverge again)
Stop loss 2 ATR from entry

Subjective Divergence Analysis. Subjective divergence analysis
typically involves comparing the peaks and troughs of the two time
series under consideration. A negative or bearish divergence is said t
rule_text: A trader should initiate a position when price action in one series diverges from a confirming indicator series, signaling weakening momentum in the direction of the trend.
entry_conditions: Price forms 3+ successively higher peaks while indicator forms 3+ successively lower peaks (bearish divergence)
OR price forms 3+ successively lower troughs while indicator forms 3+ successively higher troughs (bullish divergence)
Divergence must persist across minimum 5 bars minimum between confirmed peaks/troughs
Indicator peak/trough must be lower/higher by minimum 2% relative to prior peak/trough
exit_conditions: Price confirms new high/low in direction opposite to divergence signal
Indicator confirms new high/low in alignment with price (negating divergence)
Time-based: 20 bars maximum holding period

rule_text: A trader should initiate a signal when two price series exhibit significant divergence in their channel-normalized values, quantified as the difference between one series at an extreme high normalized level and another at an extreme low normalized level.
entry_conditions: Series A channel-normalized value ≥ 85
Series B channel-normalized value ≤ 15
Difference between normalized values ≥ 70
S&P 500 is primary series (Series A)
exit_conditions: Either series crosses back toward midpoint (50)

source_citation: Evidence-Based Technical Analysis, David Aronson, Ch. Case Study, p. 436
raw_chunk_text:
In most applications of cointegration, the error-correction model is
used to predict the behavior of the spread between the cointegrated time
series. In these applications, the divergence is predicted
rule_text: When the error-correction model of cointegrated time series diverges to an extreme level, a trader should initiate a mean-reversion trade in the direction opposite to the divergence, anticipating the spread will correct back toward its normal state.
entry_conditions: Cointegration confirmed (Johansen test, p < 0.05)
Error-correction term (residual) exceeds 2.5 standard deviations from zero
Spread has persisted in extreme territory for at least 3 bars
Volume confirms the divergence move
exit_conditions: Error-correction term crosses zero (back to mean)
Stop loss at 3.5 standard deviations (hard stop against catastrophic reversion failure)

rule_text: A trader should take contrarian positions when institutional cash reserves diverge significantly from the level predicted by their linear relationship with short-term interest rates, as extreme divergence (excessive pessimism or optimism) correlates with future market reversions.

Confirm divergence persists for ≥5 consecutive trading days
exit_conditions: Exit when cash reserves return to within 1.0 standard deviations of predicted level
Exit after 60 trading days if divergence does not mean-revert

The question was how to create signaling rules from the modified di-
vergence indicator. Two rules seemed obvious: a bullish divergence rule,
which would call for long positions in the S&P 500 when th
rule_text: Enter long positions when a companion series shows strength relative to the primary series (positive divergence above upper threshold), and enter short positions when the companion series shows weakness relative to the primary series (negative divergence below lower threshold), based on the assumption that the companion series has leading information about future price direction.
entry_conditions: Divergence indicator touches or crosses above upper threshold
Divergence indicator touches or crosses below lower threshold
Entry on any bar meeting threshold condition
exit_conditions: Divergence indicator reverses by 0.5 sigma
Stop loss: 1% below entry
Profit target: 0.75% above entry (quick scalp)

rule_text: A trader should enter a position when a divergence rule detects a mismatch between price action and an indicator series, with signal strength determined by threshold displacement and normalized over a specific lookback period.
entry_conditions: Divergence type identified (bullish type 6 or bearish type 7)
Price and companion series threshold displacement ≥ 20 points
Channel normalization lookback span = 60 days
Divergence confirmed by at least 2 companion data series
exit_conditions: Price breaks opposite threshold of divergence signal
Companion series reconverges with price action

rule_text: A trader should recognize when affective (emotional) judgment is distorting their market analysis relative to the evidence, and actively apply analytical discipline to override emotional bias—particularly when negative feelings about market direction cause bullish or bearish bias that exceeds what the data supports.
entry_conditions: Trader documents objective technical/analytical signals with confidence score (0-100)
Trader rates emotional conviction separately (0-100)
Emotional_conviction - Evidence_score <= 20 percentage_points
Position only taken if analytical evidence score > 60
exit_conditions: Exit if evidence score drops below 40
Exit if emotional conviction exceeds evidence by > 30 percentage_points

entry_conditions: Calculate emotional conviction score (0-100) and evidence score (0-100)

n order to compensate for market bias interacting with long-short bias,
one should compute the expected return of a random system with the
same long-short bias, and subtract this bias-related return
rule_text: Subtract the expected return of a random system with identical long-short bias from a candidate system's return to isolate true alpha and remove bias-driven returns.
entry_conditions: Candidate system generates signal (long/short/neutral)
Calculate position bias ratio: (# long positions / total positions)
Generate random system expectancy using identical bias ratio across full historical dataset
exit_conditions: If bias-adjusted alpha falls below threshold, reject or recalibrate system

rule_text: Volume sustains price momentum over medium-term horizons (3-12 months), but low-volume momentum shows superior persistence over longer horizons (3+ years); traders should weight volume differently based on their intended holding period.
entry_conditions: Price momentum > 0 (positive return over lookback period)
Volume(current period) > Volume(SMA 20) by 40% or more
Momentum persists for at least 2 consecutive periods
exit_conditions: Exit at 12 months elapsed or price momentum becomes negative
Exit if volume falls below 50th percentile

rule_text: Volume sustains price momentum over medium-term horizons (3-12 months), but low-volume momentum shows superior persistence over longer horizons (3+ years); traders should weight volume differently based on their intended holding period.
entry_conditions: Price momentum > 0 (lookback 12+ months)
Volume(current period) <= Volume(SMA 60) (low volume preferred)
Negative momentum must NOT have high volume (avoid high-volume downside persistence)
exit_conditions: Exit at 36 months or if 12-month momentum becomes negative
Exit immediately if volume spikes above 75th percentile (reversal signal)

source_citation: Evidence-Based Technical Analysis, David Aronson, Ch. 1, p. 512
raw_chunk_text:
1. This is an extreme rule, Type 12, based on input series 28 (10-day moving av-
erage of the up/down volume ratio), using threshold offset of 10 (upper = 60,
lower = 40) and a channel normalization l
rule_text: A trader should take short positions when an up/down volume ratio moving average exceeds an upper threshold, and take long positions when it falls below that threshold, using channel normalization to adapt thresholds to recent volatility regime.
entry_conditions: 10-day MA of up/down volume ratio > upper_threshold → SHORT
10-day MA of up/down volume ratio <= upper_threshold → LONG
Upper threshold = 70, Lower threshold = 30 (widest bands)
Channel normalization lookback = 50 days
exit_conditions: Exit SHORT when ratio falls below upper_threshold
Exit LONG when ratio rises above upper_threshold
